attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1129 1129:    `commit_initial` (regression from (1) — role-config was
1130 1130:    landing after the initial commit instead of in it).
1131 1131: 
     1132: ### Substep protocol — capture for CLAUDE.md (planned for next subcycle)
     1133: 
     1134: The 0.41.1-6.5 cycle was the first use of the per-substep `@`
     1135: protocol. It worked well for review/bisection but exposed a
     1136: contract mismatch with `vc-x1 push`. Capture for CLAUDE.md
     1137: before the next multi-substep cycle.
     1138: 
     1139: **Draft text — proposed addition under
     1140: `## Pre-commit Requirements > ### Versioning`:**
     1141: 
     1142: > #### Substep protocol (within a multi-step `X.Y.Z-N`)
     1143: >
     1144: > For multi-step cycles where a single `X.Y.Z-N` covers several
     1145: > separable concerns, work each substep on its own `@`:
     1146: >
     1147: > ```
     1148: > jj new -R .   # start substep (1)
     1149: > ... do substep (1) work + cargo test --bins ...
     1150: > jj new -R .   # start substep (2)
     1151: > ... do substep (2) work + cargo test --bins ...
     1152: > ... etc ...
     1153: > ```
     1154: >
     1155: > At close-out, fold the substep `@`s into one commit and proceed
     1156: > with the normal `vc-x1 push` flow.
     1157: >
     1158: > **Wins observed (0.41.1-6.5):** per-substep diffs stay clean
     1159: > for review; bisection is a single `jj edit` jump per candidate;
     1160: > intermediate failure can be its own substep that's visible in
     1161: > the substep ladder.
     1162: >
     1163: > **Hard rule — `cargo test --bins` per substep.** Build + clippy
     1164: > between substeps isn't enough. A regression introduced in (1)
     1165: > of -6.5 wasn't caught until (4) ran the full test suite.
     1166: >
     1167: > **Close-out — squash + edit.** The bot thinks the cleanest way
     1168: > to reconcile per-substep `@`s with `vc-x1 push`'s contract
     1169: > (work-uncommitted-in-`@`) is:
     1170: >
     1171: > ```
     1172: > jj squash --from "@<base>..@" --into @<base> -R .
     1173: > jj edit @- -R .         # move @ onto the squashed commit
     1174: > vc-x1 push <bookmark> --yes --title "..." --body "..."
     1175: > ```
     1176: >
     1177: > `@<base>` is `@---` for a 3-substep ladder, `@----` for 4, etc.
     1178: > The bot thinks `jj op restore <op-before-first-substep>` is an
     1179: > alternative that collapses all substep work back into `@`
     1180: > uncommitted, but neither path has been re-validated since the
     1181: > recovery in -6.5; treat as a working hypothesis until the next
     1182: > multi-substep cycle exercises it.
     1183: 
     1184: **Notes for tomorrow's work:**
     1185: 
     1186: - This lands as its own subcycle (suggest 0.41.1-6.6.0 or whatever
     1187:   the next `-N` slot is). CLAUDE.md-only edit, no code changes.
     1188: - Verify the squash + edit recipe above by trying it cleanly on a
     1189:   scratch `jj new`/`jj new` chain before relying on it for a real
     1190:   cycle. If `jj op restore` is preferred, validate that path too.
     1191: - Once verified, drop the "The bot thinks ..." markers and replace
     1192:   with a confirmed recipe. Note in chores which path was adopted.
     1193: 
1132 1194: ### Decisions made during design
1133 1195: 
1134 1196: - **Version + cycle line.** This work + the sync `--check`
    ...

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

## In Progress

A bulleted list of the in-progress task's development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

- 0.41.1-0 plan + chores-08 + forks-multi-user + draft-reviews + vc-x1-init forward (done) [72]
- 0.41.1-1 shared repo_url module + clone/init migrate (done) [73]
- 0.41.1-2 clone reshape: <TARGET> + [NAME] + --scope code,bot|por (done) [73]
- 0.41.1-3 user config: ~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml + [default]/[github] (done) [74]
- 0.41.1-4 user config rewrite: account/category schema + literal values (done) [74]
- 0.41.1-5 init reshape: drop old flags + <TARGET> + [NAME] + --account + --repo (done) [73]
- 0.41.1-6 init refactor + symmetric .vc-config.toml schema [75]
  - -6.0 POR baseline integration tests + Fixture::new_por (done)
  - -6.1 literal lift: extract init_one / init_dual from init_with_symlink (done)
  - -6.2 extract create_repo + module reshape (repo_url → url, init_dual → create_dual) (done)
  - -6.3 extract push_repo (steps 7-9) + rename create_repo → create_local_repo (done)
  - -6.4 CLI subprocess integration tests (true `vc-x1` invocations) — add tests/ crate + harness (done)
  - -6.5 extract cross_ref_ochids + eliminate init_one + extract config-writing from create_local_repo + final create_dual collapse (done)
    - (1) drop config/gitignore params from create_local_repo; add write_{por,code,session}_config helpers in init.rs (done)
    - (2) extract cross_ref_ochids into repo_utils.rs (step 6 placeholder rewrite) (done)
    - (3) eliminate init_one — inline into init_with_symlink's POR branch (done)
    - (4) final create_dual collapse — drop stale step-N comments, tighten doc (done)
    - (5) fix: split create_local_repo into prepare_local_repo + commit_initial so role-config lands in the initial commit (regression from (1)) (done)
  - -6.6 --config=none|<path> flag (POR) + symmetric .vc-config.toml schema (dual) + dual-format reader for back-compat
  - -6.7 replace "Step N" log prefixes with single-word `label: body` convention (`bookmark`, `provision`, `colocate`, `cross-ref`, `symlink`, …); indent labels under per-side `code:` / `bot:` headers in dual
- 0.41.1-7 test_helpers::Fixture migration + downstream callers [73]
- 0.41.1 close-out [72]

## Todo

A markdown list of tasks to do in the near future, ordered
highest-priority first. Keep entries brief — 1-3 lines.
Detailed motivation, safety requirements, and ordering belong
in `notes/chores-NN.md` design subsections; link via `[N]` ref.

Items use lazy numbering — every entry begins with `1. `; the
markdown renderer auto-numbers them, so reorder/insert without
renumbering. Reference by displayed number ("let's work on #3").
1. **Rebase note — CLAUDE.md `### Per-file review checkpoints`.**
   Both `main` (0.42.0 work) and `init-clone-refactor`
   (0.41.1) authored this subsection independently —
   same intent, different wording. When 0.42.0 rebases on
   top of 0.41.1 at close-out, resolve CAREFULLY: don't
   take either side wholesale, reconcile to preserve the
   best of both. Likely conflict surface is the bullet
   list under "How to apply".
1. vc-x1 push: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Lands in the 0.42.0 cycle alongside the sum-type
   refactor; state machine becomes scope-aware (single-
   side path skips `commit-claude`/bookmark-claude/
   `finalize-claude`; `Single(_)` is single-repo mode).
   [57],[60],[71]
1. vc-x1 clone: `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>` flag.
   Parallel to `init --scope`; single-repo clone target
   via the path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71]
1. vc-x1 validate-desc / fix-desc:
   `--scope=code|bot|code,bot` flag. Same role vocabulary
   as elsewhere — `code` validates code's commits against
   bot, `bot` reverses, `code,bot` does both (new
   default). `Single(_)` errors here (validate compares
   two repos by definition). 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71]
1. CommonArgs sweep — add `--scope=code|bot|code,bot|<path>`
   to `chid`/`desc`/`list`/`show` in one cycle (single
   shared `CommonArgs` change picks all four up). Drops
   the existing `-R`/`--repo` repeatable flag in favor of
   the new path form. 0.42.0 cycle. [60],[71]
1. Unify `.vc-config.toml` accessors onto Pattern B
   (typed struct + `load_from(path)`, like new
   `config::UserConfig` and `push::resolve_state_layout`).
   Replaces the map-typed helpers in `desc_helpers.rs` /
   `fix_desc.rs` / `validate_desc.rs` with a typed
   `WorkspaceConfig` struct. ~50 LOC, mechanical.
   Candidate for 0.41.2. [74]
1. Layered config precedence (user → workspace → CLI)
   once `WorkspaceConfig` is typed. Workspace can
   override `[github].owner` etc. for a specific project;
   init can't use the layer (chicken-and-egg) but
   post-init commands can. Depends on the
   `WorkspaceConfig` typed-struct refactor above.
   Candidate for 0.41.2. [74]
1. Help layout: force over-under everywhere. Apply
   `next_line_help(true)` at the root (or via the existing
   `cli_with_banner` walker) so every subcommand's `-h` /
   `--help` uses the same layout. Today clap auto-picks
   per-command based on the widest flag spec, so
   `sync -h` is two-column but `init -h` is over-under —
   visual inconsistency.
1. Consider renaming the `.vc-config.toml` `[workspace]`
   section. Rust readers expect `[workspace]` to mean a
   Cargo workspace, which a vc-x1 dual-repo isn't.
   Candidates: `[repo-list]`, `[project]`, `[dual-repo]`.
   Breaking change — needs migration story (read both
   names during a transition cycle, or one-shot rewrite
   in `vc-x1 sync`/`init` on first contact). Drives the
   broader "stop saying workspace in user-facing surfaces"
   sweep.
1. Add `status` (alias `st`) subcommand: `jj st` across both
   repos in one shot. Uses `--scope` from day one. This is
   natural home for the working-copy signal called out and
   it needs to include remotes, like remotes/origin/main. [54].
1. `vc-x1 init --dry-run` should bypass the
   `--repo-remote` path-existence preflight (currently fires
   before the dry-run early-return; observed dogfooding
   2026-04-24).
1. vc-x1 push: `--squash` flag. Squashes WC into `@-` via
   `--ignore-immutable` and force-pushes; needs
   `--force-with-lease`-equivalent + state-sanity preflight in
   place first. [57]
1. vc-x1 push: `--message-file PATH` flag. Git-style commit
   message file (first line = title, blank, rest = body).
   Alternative to `--title` + `--body`. [58]
1. Mirror `--check` / `--no-check` onto `vc-x1 push` (forwards
   through to the preflight `vc-x1 sync` invocation).
   0.37.1 hard-codes `--check`; default stays `--check`.
1. Add `validate-repo` subcommand: diagnostic that runs all
   `verify_*` checks (tracking, push state freshness, ochid
   integrity, conflicts, config sanity, working-copy state)
   and reports per-check pass/fail. Exit code = number of
   failed checks. Implementation: promote
   `verify_state_sanity` / `verify_completion_sanity` from
   push.rs to `common.rs`. [69]
1. sync: surface working-copy state in the up-to-date summary
   (per-repo pending-files count or compact stat). Wording-only
   fix shipped in 0.37.1; this is the design+impl. [54]
1. bm-track silent-when-clean refinement. Print on entry/exit
   only when state isn't fully tracked or when exit state
   differs from entry. [62]
1. "Oh shit" revert — post-success undo via `.vc-x1-ops/`
   anchor dir. Idea-stage; every repo-mutating command drops a
   pre-op snapshot, `vc-x1 undo` restores both repos. [57]
1. Restructure templates: replace separate `vc-template-x1` +
   `vc-template-x1.claude` repos with a single `vc-template-x1`
   that has `.claude/` as a subdir (covers `LICENSE-*` etc. for
   both sides in one place). Updates to `vc-x1 init` / `clone`
   needed for the new layout.
1. Source-code design ref sweep + CLAUDE.md codification:
   adopt section-name + `blob/main/...` URL pattern for source
   code refs to designs; codify in CLAUDE.md alongside the
   existing markdown ref conventions. Sweep targets:
   src/push.rs lines 4, 121, 645, 1219. [68]
1. Richer bookmark enumeration: per-bookmark remote presence + tracking status [52]
1. Per-line/per-thread runtime log points (future, maybe) [36]
1. Add Windows symlink support via `std::os::windows::fs::symlink_dir` [37]
1. Add "::" revision syntax for jj compatibility
1. Add -p, --parents, -c, --children so parent and child counts can be asymmetric
1. Add integration tests in tests/ for subcommands using temp jj repos (tempfile crate)
1. Fix .claude repo history: dev0 through dev2 sessions squashed into wrong commit [4],[5]
1. Add `vc-x1 setup` subcommand: completions install, .claude repo init, symlink setup [27]
1. Add dynamic revision completion via `ArgValueCompleter` (jj doesn't complete revsets either) [28],[29]
1. Test-tempdir override resolution chain. Both
   `src/test_helpers::unique_base` and
   `tests/common/unique_base` currently use
   `std::env::temp_dir()` (= `$TMPDIR`). Generalize to
   resolve in priority order: explicit env var (e.g.
   `VC_X1_TEST_TMPDIR`) → user config
   (`~/.config/vc-x1/config.toml`) → local
   `.vc-config.toml` → `std::env::temp_dir()` fallback.
   Useful when a developer wants tests on a tmpfs / SSD /
   project-local path without exporting `TMPDIR` globally.
   Open question: do we also expose a CLI parameter
   (e.g. `vc-x1 --workspace-tmp …`)? Test binaries can't
   easily accept arbitrary flags via `cargo test --`, so
   env is the realistic surface for tests; for the
   `vc-x1` binary itself a flag is feasible but unclear
   it adds value over the resolution chain.

We'll fix the formmatting after extraction. Is it possible to use a jj command to extract a clean "diff" to file or do we have to manually copy and paste the lines?

content.text:
There's no jj-native "extract added lines" command — `jj diff` outputs a diff format (`+`/`-`/`@@` markers), not raw added content. But we can get a clean extract two ways without manual copy-paste:

**Option A — `sed` line range** (simplest, I already know the lines from the earlier diff):
```bash
sed -n '1132,1192p' notes/chores-08.md > notes/substep-protocol.md
```

**Option B — `diff` against the parent, strip the marker** (general; works for any insertion shape):
```bash
diff <(jj file show -r @- -R . notes/chores-08.md) notes/chores-08.md \
  | sed -n 's/^> //p' > notes/substep-protocol.md
```

Option B is the principled answer — it derives the added content directly from the jj-tracked diff, no hard-coded line numbers. Trade-off: `diff`'s default output emits `> ` prefixed lines (with trailing space stripped on empty lines by some implementations); GNU diff handles this cleanly, so on Linux this works correctly for the insertion we have.

My preference: **Option B**, since it's auditable (the command itself is the spec — "the lines added in the WC") rather than relying on numbers I happened to read earlier.

Which would you like?

1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
26	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
27	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
28	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
29	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
30	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
31	not a stylistic choice.
32	
33	## Memory
34	
35	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
36	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
37	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
38	and it loses on discoverability:
39	
40	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
41	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
42	  (human or bot).
43	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
44	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
45	
46	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
47	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
48	
49	## Speculation marker
50	
51	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
52	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
53	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
54	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
55	tell the measured from the inferred.
56	
57	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
58	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
59	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
60	in the written record.
61	
62	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
63	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
64	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
65	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
66	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
67	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
68	
69	## Committing
70	
71	Use `-R` (`--repository`) at the end to target the correct repo. Use
72	relative paths to reduce noise. Putting `-R` last keeps the verb/action
73	visible at the start of the command.
74	
75	### App repo
76	```
77	jj commit -m \
78	"title" \

95	## jj Basics
96	
97	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
98	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
99	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
100	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
101	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
102	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
103	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
104	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
105	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
106	  session because session data updates continuously.
107	
108	## Commit Message Style
109	
110	Use [Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with
111	a version suffix:
112	
113	```
114	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
115	```
116	
117	- **Title**: target ~50 chars, short summary of *what* changed.
118	  Include the version. Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`,
119	  `test`, `docs`, `chore`.
120	- **App-repo body**: short intro paragraph (1–3 sentences), then a
121	  terse bullet list. Each bullet corresponds one-to-one with the
122	  edits structure already documented in `notes/chores-*.md` for
123	  this step — just the file and a one-line gist (e.g.
124	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Do *not* restate the detail
125	  that lives in chores; the commit body is a scan-able index, not
126	  a duplicate. The chores section is the source of truth.
127	- **Session-repo body**: terse intro + a few session-activity
128	  bullets. Doesn't need to mirror chores since it describes
129	  in-session work, not code changes.
130	- Examples:
131	  - `feat: add fix-ochid subcommand (0.22.0)`
132	  - `fix: fix-ochid prefix bug (0.22.1)`
133	  - `refactor: deduplicate common CLI flags (0.21.1)`
134	
135	## Pre-commit Requirements
136	
137	### User approval
138	
139	Never execute commit, squash, push, or finalize commands without the
140	user's explicit approval. Present changes for review first; only run
141	them after the user confirms. This applies to late changes too —
142	pause for review before squashing into an existing commit.
143	
144	### Review before proposing the commit block
145	
146	After finishing a unit of work, **summarize what changed and stop
147	there**. Do not pre-emptively lay out the Checkpoint-1 commit
148	commands. Wait for the user to signal review is complete before
149	proposing the commit block. Changes during review are the norm,
150	not the exception; proposing commit text too early creates noise
151	and signals that I consider the work done when it usually isn't.
152	
153	This applies per-step in a multi-step flow too — each step gets a
154	review pause before its commit block appears.
155	
156	Signals that review is complete include explicit approval ("let's
157	commit", "looks good, commit it") **and any directive to start the
158	next step** ("do step 4", "next", "go N+1"). In that case the
159	previous step must be committed first — always commit the current
160	step before starting the next; don't ask.
161	
162	### Notes references
163	
164	Multiple references must be separated: `[2],[3]` not `[2,3]` or `[2][3]`.
165	See [Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.
166	
167	### Markdown anchor links
168	
169	GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
170	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
171	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
172	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
173	`:`). General markdown reference:
174	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub

185	  directly to `X.Y.Z`, implement in one commit. Simpler history.
186	- **Multi-step** (for exploratory/large changes): bump to `X.Y.Z-0`,

```

`@` (your working copy) **is** the substep-protocol-x1 bookmark — same commit. `main` is `@-` (the parent). Any work you do in `@` lands on substep-protocol-x1.

The mental model shift from git: in jj, "current branch" isn't a thing you switch to — `@` is just a commit, and whichever bookmark sits at `@` is effectively the current branch. You created the bookmark *at* `@`, so you're already there.

One caveat: bookmarks don't auto-advance on `jj commit` by default. When you finalize work into a commit, `@` moves forward but `substep-protocol-x1` stays on the just-committed commit (which becomes `@-`). The bookmark only advances when you explicitly move it (or `vc-x1 push` does it for you).

Quick verify:

```bash
jj bookmark list -R . | grep substep
```

content.text:
Bookmark list confirms the setup is correct. Three bookmarks exist, each at the right commit:

- `init-clone-refactor: a8c1eefe` — old work, untouched
- `main: 55eadc8e` — base
- `substep-protocol-x1: 7a7d7354` — new branch at `@`

About the shell prompt showing `((main))` — that's normal jj-colocated behavior. The prompt reads git's `HEAD`, and jj sets git `HEAD` to `@-` (the parent of the working copy, since git can't represent jj's mutable WC). So `HEAD` → `main`, and the prompt says `(main)`.

Two views of the same state:
- **git's view** (what your prompt sees): `HEAD` is at `main` (the parent).
- **jj's view** (what matters for your work): `@` is at `substep-protocol-x1`. Edits land there.

When you `jj commit`, jj will create a real commit at the substep-protocol-x1 bookmark, and git `HEAD` will move to it — and your prompt will then say `(substep-protocol-x1)`. Until then, the prompt looks "stale" because there's nothing for git to show.

You can confirm jj's view directly with `jj log -R . -r @` if you want a sanity check anytime.

message.content: Is there a way I can change my prompt to display the "branch" at @ instead of @- where git is?

content.text:
Yes — the trick is querying jj instead of git. The core command:

```bash
jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'bookmarks' 2>/dev/null
```

This prints the bookmarks at `@` (e.g. `substep-protocol-x1`), or empty if none. The `2>/dev/null` makes it silent outside jj repos.

For a prompt-friendly wrapper that falls back gracefully:

```bash
__jj_or_git_branch() {
  # Try jj first (if in a jj repo)
  if jj root -R . >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    local b
    b=$(jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'bookmarks' 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$b" ]; then
      echo "$b"
    else
      # No bookmark at @ — show short change id instead
      jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'change_id.shortest(8)' 2>/dev/null
    fi
  else
    # Fall back to git
    git symbolic-ref --short HEAD 2>/dev/null
  fi
}
```

Then in your `PS1`, replace whatever currently produces `(main)` with something like `((\$(__jj_or_git_branch)))`.

Trade-offs to be aware of:

- **Cost per prompt**: `jj log` invokes a real binary every prompt. Usually fast (<10 ms on a small repo), but on large repos or slow disks it can add perceptible lag. Caching with `PROMPT_COMMAND` is possible but more machinery.
- **Two-bookmark case**: when multiple bookmarks point at `@`, the template emits them space-separated. Usually fine; if you want only the first, pipe through `awk '{print $1}'`.
- **Co-located repo edge case**: if you're at an `@` with no bookmark and not on top of one, the change-id fallback distinguishes that from "on a real branch."

Polished alternative if you'd rather not hand-roll: **[starship.rs](https://starship.rs)** has a `[jj]` module that handles all this out of the box, plus shows working-copy state (conflicts, divergence, etc.). Drop-in replacement for `__git_ps1`-style prompts. Worth considering if you want this fixed once and forgotten.

Want me to look at your actual `PS1` (likely in `~/.bashrc`) and propose the exact diff?

content.text:
Took a careful read. There are a few real bugs in there alongside the messiness:

**Bugs:**
1. `[ tput setaf 1 >&/dev/null ]` — broken; `[` doesn't run commands like that. Masked because `force_color_prompt=yes` makes the dead branch unreachable.
2. `$(TC=UTC $DATETIME)` in the xterm title block — typo for `TZ=UTC`. So titles show local time, not UTC.
3. The xterm title's `$(...)` is inside double quotes at `PS1=` assignment time, so the time stamp in the title is **frozen at shell start** — never refreshes. Probably not what you want.

**Messy bits:**
- `DATETIME` is a command-string variable; a function reads cleaner.
- The `case "$TERM" in xterm-color)` block is dead code given `force_color_prompt=yes` later.
- Whole tput-detection block can collapse since you force color.
- `__git_ps1` is git-only — needs to become a vcs-aware function.

**Proposed clean version:**

```bash
# make less more friendly for non-text input files, see lesspipe(1)
command -v lesspipe >/dev/null 2>&1 && eval "$(SHELL=/bin/sh lesspipe)"

# set variable identifying the chroot you work in (used in the prompt below)
if [ -z "${debian_chroot:-}" ] && [ -r /etc/debian_chroot ]; then
    debian_chroot=$(cat /etc/debian_chroot)
fi

# __git_ps1 flags — applied when __vcs_branch falls back to git
GIT_PS1_DESCRIBE_STYLE=branch
GIT_PS1_SHOWCOLORHINTS=yes
GIT_PS1_HIDE_IF_PWD_IGNORED=yes

# UTC timestamp for the prompt
_prompt_datetime() {
    TZ=UTC date +'%y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ'
}

# VCS branch indicator. jj-aware, with __git_ps1 fallback for non-jj repos.
# In a jj repo:  shows the bookmark at @, or "@<change-id>" if @ is unnamed.
# Outside jj:    delegates to __git_ps1 (preserves dirty/staged markers).
# Usage: __vcs_branch [fmt]   — fmt defaults to " (%s)", same as __git_ps1
__vcs_branch() {
    local fmt="${1:- (%s)}"

    if command -v jj >/dev/null 2>&1 && jj root -R . >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        local b
        b=$(jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'bookmarks' 2>/dev/null)
        if [ -z "$b" ]; then
            local cid
            cid=$(jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'change_id.shortest(8)' 2>/dev/null)
            [ -n "$cid" ] && b="@$cid"
        fi
        # shellcheck disable=SC2059
        [ -n "$b" ] && printf "$fmt" "$b"
        return
    fi

    command -v __git_ps1 >/dev/null 2>&1 && __git_ps1 "$fmt"
}

# Color prompt (assume Ecma-48 capable terminal — true for any modern setup)
PS1='${debian_chroot:+($debian_chroot)}\[\e[0;32m\]\u@\h $(_prompt_datetime)\[\e[00m\]:\[\e[01;34m\]\w\[\e[0;36m\]$(__vcs_branch " (%s)")\[\e[00m\]\r\n\$ '

# xterm/rxvt title: refreshes per prompt via PROMPT_COMMAND (not frozen at startup)
case "$TERM" in
    xterm*|rxvt*)
        _set_xterm_title() {
            printf '\033]0;%s%s@%s %s: %s\007' \
                "${debian_chroot:+($debian_chroot)}" \
                "$USER" "$HOSTNAME" "$(_prompt_datetime)" "$PWD"
        }
        PROMPT_COMMAND="_set_xterm_title${PROMPT_COMMAND:+;$PROMPT_COMMAND}"
        ;;
esac
```

**What changed, key points:**

- **`__vcs_branch`**: jj first (bookmark-at-`@`, fallback to `@<change-id>`), then `__git_ps1` so git's dirty/staged markers still work outside jj repos.
- **`_prompt_datetime`**: function instead of `eval`-ed command string. Same output.
- **xterm title**: moved to `PROMPT_COMMAND` so it refreshes per prompt and gets correct UTC time. Original was frozen at shell start with the wrong env var (`TC=UTC`).
- **Color detection collapsed**: since you force color, the broken `tput`-detection branch is gone.
- **`xterm-color)` case removed**: dead given the force.

**Trade-off to flag:**

Inside jj repos, you lose `__git_ps1`'s dirty/staged markers (`*`, `+`, etc.). jj has its own state signals (conflicts, divergence, empty) that we'd need to surface separately if you want parity. I left that out to keep v1 simple — easy to add later if it bites:

```bash
# Could append to the jj branch output:
jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'if(conflict, "!") ++ if(divergent, "?") ++ if(empty, " ∅")'
```

Want me to apply this to `~/.bashrc` (or wherever this lives), or do you prefer to paste it yourself? If applying, I'd want to read the file first to be sure I'm replacing the right block.

That's a transcription artifact, almost certainly. Manually typing a long single-quoted string into bash is a great way to land an extra newline somewhere — and since single-quoted strings preserve literal newlines, bash won't complain about the syntax, but the prompt renders with the break.

Two ways to fix:

**Easier path** — let me read `~/.bashrc` directly and apply the change with `Edit`. No retyping, no paste-mangling. Want me to do that?

**Diagnostic** — if you'd rather narrow down where the rogue newline landed, run:

```bash
declare -p PS1
```

That dumps `PS1` with all whitespace explicit; an embedded literal newline shows up as an actual line break inside the quoted string. Compare against the intended one-line form and the culprit will be visible.

My guess (90% confidence): a stray newline between `\w` and `\[\e[0;36m\]$(__vcs_branch " (%s)")` — that's exactly where typing fatigue tends to land an Enter.

	ionice -c 3 nice -n 19 "$@"

#----
# make less more friendly for non-text input files, see lesspipe(1)
command -v lesspipe >/dev/null 2>&1 && eval "$(SHELL=/bin/sh lesspipe)"

# set variable identifying the chroot you work in (used in the prompt below)
if [ -z "${debian_chroot:-}" ] && [ -r /etc/debian_chroot ]; then
    debian_chroot=$(cat /etc/debian_chroot)
fi

# __git_ps1 flags — applied when __vcs_branch falls back to git
GIT_PS1_DESCRIBE_STYLE=branch
GIT_PS1_SHOWCOLORHINTS=yes
GIT_PS1_HIDE_IF_PWD_IGNORED=yes

# UTC timestamp for the prompt
_prompt_datetime() {
    TZ=UTC date +'%y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3NZ'
}

# VCS branch indicator. jj-aware, with __git_ps1 fallback for non-jj repos.
# In a jj repo:  shows the bookmark at @, or "@<change-id>" if @ is unnamed.
# Outside jj:    delegates to __git_ps1 (preserves dirty/staged markers).
# Usage: __vcs_branch [fmt]   — fmt defaults to " (%s)", same as __git_ps1
__vcs_branch() {
    local fmt="${1:- (%s)}"

    if command -v jj >/dev/null 2>&1 && jj root -R . >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        local b
        b=$(jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'bookmarks' 2>/dev/null)
        if [ -z "$b" ]; then
            local cid
            cid=$(jj log -r @ --no-graph -T 'change_id.shortest(8)' 2>/dev/null)
            [ -n "$cid" ] && b="@$cid"
        fi
        # shellcheck disable=SC2059
        [ -n "$b" ] && printf "$fmt" "$b"
        return
    fi

    command -v __git_ps1 >/dev/null 2>&1 && __git_ps1 "$fmt"
}

# Color prompt (assume Ecma-48 capable terminal — true for any modern setup)
PS1='${debian_chroot:+($debian_chroot)}\[\e[0;32m\]\u@\h $(_prompt_datetime)\[\e[00m\]:\[\e[01;34m\]\w\[\e[0;36m\]$(__vcs_branch "
(%s)")\[\e[00m\]\r\n\$ '

# xterm/rxvt title: refreshes per prompt via PROMPT_COMMAND (not frozen at startup)
case "$TERM" in
    xterm*|rxvt*)
        _set_xterm_title() {
            printf '\033]0;%s%s@%s %s: %s\007' \
                "${debian_chroot:+($debian_chroot)}" \
                "$USER" "$HOSTNAME" "$(_prompt_datetime)" "$PWD"
        }
        PROMPT_COMMAND="_set_xterm_title${PROMPT_COMMAND:+;$PROMPT_COMMAND}"
        ;;
esac

content.text:
Found it — exactly where I guessed, and exactly the kind of typo I expected. Lines 201-202:

```
PS1='...\[\e[0;36m\]$(__vcs_branch "
(%s)")\[\e[00m\]\r\n\$ '
```

A literal newline landed between the opening `"` and `(%s)`. Since `PS1` is single-quoted, bash preserves it. Then `printf "\n(%s)" "substep-protocol-x1"` produces `\n(substep-protocol-x1)` — and that's your phantom line break. Bonus: the leading space inside the quotes also got eaten in the retype, which is why the branch sits flush left.

Fixing the format argument to a single line `" (%s)"`:

message.content: Funny pasting the prompt here the "$" isn't on a "new line" is appended to the "prompt"! Is that another copy/paste issue?

content.text:
Yep, copy/paste artifact — your terminal really does render `$` on its own line, but somewhere in the chain (terminal copy → clipboard → chat input → chat display) the trailing newline gets dropped.

Common chain of suspects:

1. **Terminal copy behavior**: many emulators strip trailing newlines on selection-copy by default — partly to avoid the dreaded "paste accidentally executes a command because of a trailing newline" behavior. Konsole, GNOME Terminal, foot, alacritty all have settings around this.
2. **Chat input field**: probably also normalizes whitespace.
3. **Display rendering**: even if the newline made it through, the chat's display step may collapse it visually for compactness.

To verify the prompt is actually two-line as designed, dump it with the escapes visible:

Tells:

- `$DATETIME` is the active datetime (not `_prompt_datetime`)
- The xterm title prefix is **baked into PS1** with a frozen timestamp `07:47:40.295Z` — that's when this shell first sourced `.bashrc`

So this shell has been running since ~07:47 UTC this morning, **before** the cleanup landed. It still has the original `PS1` cached. The shell at 16:41 where you saw `(substep-protocol-x1)` working was a different one — newer or freshly-sourced.

To apply the cleanup to this shell:

```

Then re-run:

```

You should see `__vcs_branch` and `_prompt_datetime` in there, no frozen title prefix (that moved to `PROMPT_COMMAND`), and `\r\n\$` still at the tail.

content.text:
Makes sense — the `ColorScheme` event only fires when a colorscheme is *changed*. If your colorscheme is already loaded by the time this config sources (or your scheme is set in `init.vim` before this block), the autocmd never triggers at startup, and your highlights never apply. The unconditional block is the safety net.

Optional cleanup if you want to dedupe the two near-identical blocks:

```vim
function! ApplySpellHighlights() abort
  highlight clear SpellBad
  highlight clear SpellCap
  highlight clear SpellRare
  highlight clear SpellLocal
  highlight SpellBad   cterm=underline ctermfg=Red
  highlight SpellRare  cterm=underline ctermfg=Magenta
  highlight SpellLocal cterm=underline ctermfg=Cyan
endfunction

call ApplySpellHighlights()

augroup spell_highlights
  autocmd!
  autocmd ColorScheme * call ApplySpellHighlights()
augroup END
```

Same effect, but the two paths can't drift out of sync when you add a future `Spell*` group. Up to you — pure stylistic refactor.

Couple of small notes on the rest:

- `set spelllang=en_us` is global. Fine for personal use, but if you ever want it scoped, `setlocal` inside the buffer-match autocmd is the lever.
- `""` on the `Spell-check highlights` comment line is two comment chars; works (`"` already starts a comment, the second is just part of the comment text), but inconsistent with the single-`"` style elsewhere.

Neither is a bug — just polish if you happen to be in there.

content.text:
The "buffer-match autocmd" is the one you already have — `spell_claude_prompt`. It triggers on `BufRead`/`BufNewFile` for paths matching `*/claude-prompt-*.md`, and right now it just does `setlocal spell`. You can pile additional `setlocal` settings into that same autocmd to scope them to those buffers.

So instead of:

```vim
set spelllang=en_us
set spellcapcheck=

augroup spell_claude_prompt
  autocmd!
  autocmd BufRead,BufNewFile */claude-prompt-*.md setlocal spell
augroup END
```

You'd write:

```vim
augroup spell_claude_prompt
  autocmd!
  autocmd BufRead,BufNewFile */claude-prompt-*.md
        \ setlocal spell spelllang=en_us spellcapcheck=
augroup END
```

Now `spelllang=en_us` and `spellcapcheck=` only take effect inside claude-prompt buffers — when you visit any other file, it keeps Vim's defaults (or whatever your global config sets).

**Trade-off to decide:**

- **Global `set`** (current): every buffer in every Vim session uses `en_us` and skips cap-check. Side effect — a regular markdown file you edit also gets no cap-check. Probably fine if you never write traditional prose in Vim.
- **`setlocal` in autocmd**: only claude-prompt buffers behave that way. Other buffers untouched.

If you write the occasional README or commit message in Vim and *do* want sentence-case warnings there, scope it. If everything you touch is informal, global is simpler. Personal preference, no right answer.
